context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 3

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 26th February, 2024
Days Covered: 04 and 05 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Sorting by Reversals [15 points]

We have a permutation of the numbers {0, 1, ..., n − 1}, and we'd like to manipulate it so it becomes sorted in increasing order. For this problem, we're only allowed to make reversal moves: from a list (a0, a1, ..., a_{n−1}), we can pick a pair of indices i < j and reverse the entire subsequence from a_i to a_j:
    (a0, ..., a_{i−1}, a_i, a_{i+1}, ..., a_{j−1}, a_j, a_{j+1}, ..., a_{n−1})
  → (a0, ..., a_{i−1}, a_j, a_{j−1}, ..., a_{i+1}, a_i, a_{j+1}, ..., a_{n−1}).
If (i, j) is an inverted pair (i.e., i < j but a_i > a_j), call this move a good reversal.

In this problem we'll show that if we start with any permutation (x0, ..., x_{n−1}) of {0, ..., n − 1} and keep making good reversal moves, then no matter which ones we choose, we will eventually terminate with a sorted sequence.

(a) [3 pts] Describe this scenario as a state machine, where the states are the permutations of {0, 1, ..., n − 1}. What are the transitions? The start state?
(b) [3 pts] What are the final states? How do you know? Conclude partial correctness: if an execution of the state machine terminates, then the list must be sorted.
(c) [6 pts] In lecture, we proved termination of swap-sort by proving that the number of inverted pairs strictly decreases as we make swaps. This is not true for this problem! For example, (9, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 0) can transition to (0, 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 9), but the former has 17 inverted pairs while the latter has 28 inverted pairs.
Even so, please find a derived variable for this problem that (i) has only nonnegative integer values and (ii) is strictly decreasing. Be sure to prove that your derived variable satisfies both properties.
    Hint: Think of (a0, ..., a_{n−1}) as digits of a base-n number.
(d) [3 pts] Use parts (b) and (c) to prove total correctness: every execution must eventually terminate with a fully sorted list.


Problem 2. A Fibonacci Sum [10 points]

Recall that the Fibonacci sequence is given by F0 = 0, F1 = 1, and Fn = F_{n−1} + F_{n−2} for n > 1, so the sequence starts with 0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, ....

(a) [8 pts] Use the perturbation method to find the closed form of the sum
    S = Σ_{n=1}^∞ Fn / c^n,
where c = 10^5. You may assume the sum converges.
    Hint: Try comparing S with S/c.
(b) [2 pts] Verify your answer with WolframAlpha (www.wolframalpha.com) or some other high-precision calculator, recalling that c = 10^5. What are the first 50 digits of your result? What is the pattern and does it make sense?


Problem 3. Integral Method [15 points]

Let f(x) := x / (5 + x)^3, and let S_n := Σ_{x=n}^∞ f(x). In this problem, we will use the integral method to estimate the infinite sum S0 to an accuracy of 1/200. In other words, we will find bounds u and v such that u ≤ S0 ≤ v and |v − u| ≤ 1/200.
Feel free to use a calculator (e.g., WolframAlpha) for algebraic/numerical computations, but be sure to justify all of your reasoning.

(a) [2 pts] Explain briefly why applying the integral method directly to S0 will not work.
(b) [3 pts] Find the smallest index i such that the sum S_i can be approximated by applying the integral method directly.
(c) [4 pts] Use your answer from the previous part to find upper and lower bounds for S0. What accuracy does your approximation achieve? (I.e., what is the difference between your upper and lower bounds?)
(d) [6 pts] Find the smallest index j such that the sum S_j can be approximated to an accuracy of 1/200 by applying the integral method directly. Use your answer to find u and v (expressed as rational numbers) such that u ≤ S0 ≤ v and |v − u| ≤ 1/200.
    Hint: j should be a single digit integer.


Problem 4. Some Sums [10 points]

Find the closed form of each of the summations below.

(a) [5 pts]  Σ_{i=0}^n (5^i − 7^i) / 13^i
(b) [5 pts]  Σ_{i=1}^n Σ_{j=1}^k (3^j − i)

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m working on Problem 1, especially part (c). I’m kind of stuck on what derived variable would always decrease under a good reversal. The hint about treating the permutation like a base-\(n\) number seems important, but I’m not seeing why yet.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point noticing the hint. Let me ask you this: if you treat the permutation (a0, a1, ..., a_{n−1}) as the digits of a base-n number, what number does the *sorted* permutation correspond to? And what can you say about any *other* permutation compared to that number?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is starting on part (c) and has noticed the hint but hasn't unpacked it yet. Rather than explaining the hint, I should ask a Socratic question that nudges them to think about what a base-n interpretation of the permutation would give them — specifically, what property a sorted permutation would have versus an unsorted one in that interpretation. I'll keep it brief and let them reason it out.

exchanges.student: I think the sorted one \((0,1,2,\dots,n-1)\) would be the base-\(n\) number with those digits in that order. My guess is that it might be the smallest possible such number, since having \(0\) first seems as small as possible, but I’m not fully sure how to argue that for every other permutation.
exchanges.tutor: Good intuition! Think about how you compare two numbers digit by digit — you look at the most significant digit first, then move right. So if two permutations differ, what does the *leftmost* position where they differ tell you about which one is larger as a base-n number?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track but needs to sharpen their reasoning. Rather than confirming or explaining, I should push them to think more carefully about what it means for one permutation to give a larger base-n number than another. A good Socratic nudge is to ask them to compare two specific permutations digit by digit, like you would compare any two numbers.

exchanges.student: Right, I should compare them lexicographically. At the first position where two permutations differ, the one with the smaller digit there is the smaller base-\(n\) number. So I think \((0,1,2,\dots,n-1)\) is the smallest permutation, because any other permutation has to differ somewhere earlier by putting a bigger number too far left.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. Now connect this to good reversals. When you make a good reversal on an inverted pair (i, j) — meaning a_i > a_j — what happens to the digits at positions i and j? Does that increase or decrease the base-n value, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly established that the sorted permutation is the unique minimum base-n value among all permutations. Now I should push them to connect this to the derived variable: they need to show that a good reversal strictly decreases the base-n value. The key question is: what does a good reversal do to the digits at positions i and j (the inverted pair), and how does that affect the base-n value?

exchanges.student: I think it decreases it, because at position \(i\) I replace \(a_i\) with \(a_j\), and \(a_j<a_i\). Since position \(i\) is the first place changed, the whole base-\(n\) number should get smaller no matter what happens later in the reversed block. Is that enough, or do I need to justify that positions before \(i\) stay the same too?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, you do need to say that — it's a key part of the argument. What *does* happen to positions before i in a reversal move?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the core insight correct and is asking a good clarifying question about whether they need to address positions before i. This is actually an important part of a rigorous proof — they should explicitly note it. I'll affirm their instinct and push them to state it clearly as part of their argument, without giving away the full proof structure.

exchanges.student: They stay exactly the same. So the first place where the old and new permutations can differ is position \(i\), and there the new digit is \(a_j\), which is smaller than the old digit \(a_i\). That means the base-\(n\) value strictly decreases.
exchanges.tutor: Good — that's a solid argument for property (ii). Now quickly check property (i) from the problem: why is the base-n value always a nonnegative integer?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now assembled all the key ingredients for the proof: positions before i are unchanged, position i decreases, so the base-n value strictly decreases. They also established earlier that the sorted permutation is the unique minimum. Now I should push them to verify the two required properties from the problem statement: (i) nonneg integer values and (ii) strictly decreasing. They've shown (ii); I should prompt them to also address (i) explicitly before moving on.

exchanges.student: Because the digits are from \(\{0,\dots,n-1\}\), so the value is a valid base-\(n\) numeral. That makes it an integer, and it’s nonnegative since all the place values and digits are nonnegative.
exchanges.tutor: You've got everything you need for part (c). I'd encourage you to write that up into a clean proof now. Once you're done, part (d) asks you to use parts (b) and (c) together — do you want to think about how they combine to prove total correctness?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now addressed both required properties clearly and correctly. They have all the ingredients for a complete answer to part (c). Since we've now spent several exchanges on part (c) and they have everything they need, I should acknowledge this and nudge them toward writing up their solution and/or moving to part (d), which uses parts (b) and (c) together.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I want to try that. I think the idea is: from (c), the derived variable is a nonnegative integer that strictly decreases every move, so the process can’t go on forever. Then from (b), if it stops, it has to be sorted. So every execution terminates and ends in the sorted list. Is that basically the whole total correctness argument?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's exactly the structure. Now make sure your writeup spells out *why* a strictly decreasing nonneg integer sequence must terminate — that step is worth stating explicitly. Can you articulate that reason precisely?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the two-part structure of the total correctness argument: termination from (c) and partial correctness from (b). This is accurate and complete at the conceptual level. I should affirm this and push them to write it up carefully, since the rigor of the written proof is what matters for the assignment.

exchanges.student: I think it’s the well-ordering idea: there can’t be an infinite strictly decreasing sequence of nonnegative integers, since \(\mathbb{N}\) is well-ordered / has a minimum. So after finitely many good reversals, no transition is possible, meaning the machine terminates. Then by part (b), that final state must be sorted.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — well-ordering is the key principle, and you've stated it cleanly. You have everything for a complete proof of part (d). Write it up and you're done with Problem 1. Ready to move on to one of the other problems?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now fully and correctly articulated the total correctness argument, invoking well-ordering explicitly. This is exactly right. I should affirm this clearly and encourage them to write it up. No more scaffolding is needed on Problem 1 — I can prompt them to move on to the other problems if they're ready.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I’m ready. Maybe Problem 3 next? I’m not confident about when the integral method can be applied directly, and part (a) looks a little confusing.
exchanges.tutor: Good choice. Before worrying about S0 specifically, tell me: what are the conditions that f(x) needs to satisfy for the integral method to apply directly?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is moving to Problem 3 part (a). The key issue is understanding the conditions under which the integral method applies — specifically that f(x) must be non-negative and monotonically decreasing. I should ask them what those conditions are rather than stating them, to get them thinking about it themselves.

exchanges.student: I think \(f\) needs to be positive, continuous, and decreasing on the interval starting at the index of the sum. I’m slightly unsure if “decreasing” has to be strict, but I think monotone decreasing is enough.
exchanges.tutor: Good — monotone decreasing is enough. Now apply that to f(x) = x/(5+x)^3 starting at x = 0. Is f decreasing right from the start?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the conditions. Now I should push them to apply these conditions to f(x) = x/(5+x)^3 specifically for S0, which starts at x=0. The issue is that f is not decreasing at x=0 (it increases initially before decreasing), so I want them to discover this themselves.

grade.overview: The tutor conducted an exemplary Socratic session throughout all 10 turns. Key ideas were consistently elicited from the student rather than given directly; the tutor asked targeted questions at each step and only confirmed or refined after the student produced the reasoning. No submission-ready content was provided, no rewriting occurred, and the dialogue progressed cleanly without repetition or drift.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave the derived variable, the proof structure, or the well-ordering argument directly — each was drawn out through questions (Turns 1–8). The student generated every key idea. No near-submission-ready output was produced. 1.2: The tutor built on each correct student step (e.g., confirming lexicographic reasoning before moving to reversals, then to property (i), then to part (d)), and diagnosed understanding before advancing. 1.3: The tutor gave explicit process-level guidance, e.g., pointing out that positions before i must be addressed and that well-ordering must be stated explicitly in the writeup. 2.1: No repetitive cycling; each turn advanced the argument. 2.2: The conversation stayed tightly anchored to Problem 1 parts (c)/(d) and then transitioned cleanly to Problem 3. 3.1: Responses were concise, single-question turns throughout. 3.2: Tone was consistently supportive and coaching-oriented with no grading language.
